serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Bee Hoon Goreng Chicken weighs approximately 450 g, representing a typical single-person hawker or food-court serving in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Health Promotion Board Singapore — Nutrient Composition of Food (HPB Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore database entry for fried bee hoon / fried rice vermicelli with chicken used as primary reference. Per-100g values from input record (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) are consistent with HPB published ranges for similar fried noodle dishes and are retained with medium confidence. Energy 151 kcal/100g, protein 10.0 g, fat 6.2 g, carbohydrate 14.4 g are plausible for a chicken-enriched fried vermicelli dish. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and water data not available in the HPB entry for this dish category and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Foster-Powell, Holt, Brand-Miller; and University of Sydney GI Database — rice vermicelli analogous entry
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Bee Hoon Goreng Chicken as a composite dish. GI estimated at 58 (medium) based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for rice vermicelli (bee hoon), which reports GI values in the range 53–61 depending on preparation. The composite dish GI is moderated slightly below plain rice vermicelli due to the presence of protein (chicken, egg ~10 g/100g) and fat (6.2 g/100g), which slow gastric emptying and blunt the glycaemic response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (58 × 64.98) / 100 ≈ 37.7, rounded to 37.5. Carbohydrate per 450 g serving = 14.44 × 4.5 = 64.98 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 400 mg/100g; a 450 g serving delivers approximately 1800 mg sodium, representing 78–90% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce, fish sauce, and ketchup manis, or limit portion size to 150–200 g to keep sodium intake below 800 mg per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Rice vermicelli has a medium GI (~58) and a full 450 g serving delivers approximately 65 g of carbohydrate with a glycemic load of ~38, which is high for a single meal.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should limit portion size to 200–250 g, pair with additional non-starchy vegetables, and request reduced ketchup manis to lower the sugar and glycaemic load contribution.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 400 mg/100g is the primary cardiovascular concern; saturated fat is relatively low at 1.56 g/100g and trans fat is minimal at 0.04 g/100g, which is favourable.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should request reduced-sodium seasoning and limit portion to 200 g per meal; the low saturated fat and lean chicken protein profile are otherwise compatible with a heart-healthy diet when sodium is controlled.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Nightshade ingredients (chilli, tomato) may aggravate joint inflammation in sensitive individuals; sodium from soy sauce and fish sauce can worsen tissue swelling.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should request reduced soy sauce and fish sauce to lower sodium, and may wish to omit chilli padi and tomato-based sauces if nightshade sensitivity is a concern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bee Hoon Goreng Chicken (炒米粉鸡肉 / Bee Hoon Goreng Ayam) is a popular Singaporean and Malaysian hawker dish consisting of thin rice vermicelli noodles (bee hoon) stir-fried at high heat in a wok with sliced or shredded chicken, egg, bean sprouts, cabbage, and aromatics including garlic and chilli padi. The dish is seasoned with a combination of soy sauce, fish sauce, and ketchup manis (sweet soy sauce), giving it a characteristic savoury-sweet-spicy flavour profile with a lightly smoky wok-hei aroma. The texture is soft yet slightly chewy from the vermicelli, with tender chicken pieces and crisp bean sprouts providing contrast. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 151 kcal, 10 g protein, 6.2 g fat, and 14.4 g carbohydrate, making it a moderate-energy, protein-enriched noodle dish. Sodium content is high at 400 mg/100g, primarily from soy sauce and fish sauce, making it a concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease patients. The medium glycemic index (~58) and moderate carbohydrate content require portion control for type 2 diabetes management. The dish is typically halal and dairy-free. It is commonly served as a breakfast, lunch, or dinner item at hawker centres, food courts, and kopitiams across Singapore and Malaysia, and is a staple of the Southeast Asian noodle repertoire.
